title: Major Triads
description: A major triad is three notes: root, major third, and perfect fifth. It's the foundation of all Western harmony. Every chord, no matter how complex, traces back to this three-note structure.
practice: Build a C major triad from memory: C, E, G.
Build major triads on all 12 roots. Write them out.
Find a C major triad on the guitar in three different fretboard locations using at least two strings each.

title: Minor Triads
description: A minor triad is root, minor third, and perfect fifth. The minor third (one half-step lower than the major third) gives the chord its darker, more introspective character.
practice: Build an A minor triad: A, C, E. Compare to A major: A, C#, E.
Play a major triad, then flatten the third by one fret. Hear the quality change.
Identify whether a chord is major or minor by ear using recordings.

title: Open Chord Shapes
description: The foundational open chords — Em, Am, E, A, D, G, C — use open strings and sit in 1st position. These are the most resonant voicings on guitar and the chords in the vast majority of beginner songs.
practice: Learn all seven core open chords: Em, Am, E, A, D, G, C.
Transition between G and C in tempo — no pause between them.
Practice the 'silent chord' drill: form the chord shape without strumming. Check every string rings.

title: Power Chords
description: A power chord (root + 5th) has no third — it's neither major nor minor. This makes it work with any type of distortion without creating dissonance, which is why it's everywhere in rock.
practice: Play an E5 power chord on the 6th and 5th strings. Mute string 4 and below.
Move the same shape to A5, D5, and G5 on the low strings.
Play a I-IV-V progression using only power chords in the key of E.

title: Double Stops
description: A double stop is two notes played simultaneously on adjacent strings. They're the building block of the Smoke on the Water riff, country chicken-pickin', and countless blues fills.
practice: Play two notes on the D and G strings at the 3rd fret simultaneously. Keep both strings clean.
Play the Smoke on the Water main riff — it's built entirely from double stops.
Find a major third and a minor third double stop on the same string pair.

title: Barre Chords
description: A barre chord uses the index finger as a moveable nut, barring all six strings, with the remaining fingers forming the chord shape above it. This makes any open chord shape transposable to any key. The E-shape and A-shape are the two most essential.
practice: Play an F major chord (E-shape barre at 1st fret). Every string must ring cleanly.
Slide the E-shape barre to 5th fret (A chord) and 7th fret (B chord). Name each one.
Transition between open G and barre B (A-shape 2nd fret) 20 times in tempo.

title: Chord Inversions
description: An inversion places a chord tone other than the root in the bass. Root position: root in bass. First inversion: third in bass. Second inversion: fifth in bass. Inversions smooth out bass-line movement and add color to progressions.
practice: Play a C/E chord (C major, first inversion) with E in the bass. Compare to open C.
Voice-lead a I–IV–V progression using inversions so the bass moves by step.
Find two different inversions of G major on the neck without using a root-position voicing.

title: Dominant Seventh Chords
description: The dominant seventh chord adds a minor seventh to the major triad (root, major 3rd, perfect 5th, minor 7th). Its strong tension-and-resolution feel drives blues, jazz, and virtually every V→I cadence in Western music.
practice: Play a G7 open chord. Listen for the tension the 7th creates against the root.
Play G7 → C major. Feel how the 7th resolves.
Identify a dominant seventh chord by ear in a recording — blues songs are the best hunting ground.
